title: Turning the Database Inside-Out with Apache Samza
author: Martin Kleppmann
note: The talk behind the chapter's unbundling thesis: treat the replication log as a first-class citizen and derive materialized views from an immutable event stream instead of mutating shared state. Full video plus transcript.

title: Is Kafka a Database? (Kafka Summit keynote)
author: Martin Kleppmann
note: Kleppmann's follow-up to 'inside-out': how a totally ordered log can enforce ACID-style guarantees and consistency across heterogeneous systems. Video and slides linked from his site.

title: End-to-End Arguments in System Design
author: J.H. Saltzer, D.P. Reed, D.D. Clark
note: The classic 1984 paper the chapter applies to databases: low-level functions like duplicate suppression are redundant unless the application endpoints implement them too. Free PDF from Saltzer's MIT page.

title: Life beyond Distributed Transactions: an Apostate's Opinion
author: Pat Helland
note: Helland's CIDR 2007 position paper arguing scalable systems must abandon cross-entity transactions in favor of per-entity state plus at-least-once messaging with operation identifiers for idempotence.

title: Questioning the Lambda Architecture
author: Jay Kreps
note: The article that proposed the kappa architecture: instead of maintaining the same logic in both batch and stream layers, reprocess history by replaying a retained log through one stream processor.

title: Online Event Processing: Achieving Consistency Where Distributed Transactions Have Failed
author: Martin Kleppmann, Alastair R. Beresford, Boerge Svingen
note: CACM/Queue paper (open-access PDF linked) formalizing the chapter's log-based approach: append-only event logs provide atomicity, uniqueness constraints, and multi-partition consistency without atomic commit.

title: View Maintenance: A New Approach to Data Processing
author: Frank McSherry
note: Materialize's chief scientist frames incremental view maintenance (OLVM) as a third workload beside OLTP/OLAP, with differential dataflow doing work proportional to input changes rather than to queries.

title: Pattern: Saga
author: Chris Richardson
note: Canonical reference for sagas: a multi-service operation as a sequence of local transactions with compensating actions, via choreography or orchestration — the microservices take on multi-partition processing.
